steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Central Ideas and Details question asking for the main idea of the passage. The text makes two related claims: (1) despite the sonnet's reputation for strict formal requirements, it accommodates wide-ranging variety, and (2) poet Carl Phillips explains this by arguing that highly recognizable conventions actually invite experimentation. A correct main-idea answer must capture both the surprising variety and the reason the form's rigidity makes this possible.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "Although Anderson's and Jess's sonnets are both widely celebrated for their striking originality, most modern examples of the form are generally regarded as conventional.": The passage says nothing about most sonnets being conventional, and it does not contrast Anderson's and Jess's poems against the broader body of sonnets in terms of critical reception. ✗
  • Choice B — "As a form, the sonnet encourages a surprising amount of variety, even though certain characteristics associated with it suggest this would be unlikely.": This captures both key ideas: the sonnet's unexpected variety and the tension with its ostensibly rigid conventions. It also aligns with Phillips's point that recognizable conventions make subversion irresistible. ✓
  • Choice C — "That the sonnet remains as popular as it is today is unexpected, given that many of the features associated with the form have long since seemed antiquated to readers.": The passage does not discuss the sonnet's popularity or reader reception of its features as antiquated; the focus is on variety and experimentation, not enduring popularity. ✗
  • Choice D — "Although the sonnet is now recognized for the way it facilitates experimentation, there was a long period in its history in which very little innovation occurred.": The passage makes no claim about a historical period of stagnation; it presents the sonnet's invitation to experimentation as an inherent feature of the form, not a recent development. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
